title: Mamta Devi vs Rakesh Kumar Yadav on 15 January, 2025
body: Mamta Devi vs Rakesh Kumar Yadav on 15 January, 2025 Author: Sushrut Arvind Dharmadhikari Bench: Sushrut Arvind Dharmadhikari , Avanindra Kumar Singh NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 1 WA-3005-2024 IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT JABALPUR BEFORE HON'BLE SHRI JUSTICE SUSHRUT ARVIND DHARMADHIKARI & HON'BLE SHRI JUSTICE AVANINDRA KUMAR SINGH ON THE 15th OF JANUARY, 2025 WRIT APPEAL No. 3005 of 2024 MAMTA DEVI AND OTHERS Versus RAKESH KUMAR YADAV AND OTHERS Appearance: Shri Saket Agrawal - Learned Counsel for the appellants. Shri S.S. Chauhan - Learned Government Advocate for respondent No.10/State. ORDER Per: Justice Sushrut Arvind Dharmadhikari Heard on the question of admission. 2. Present Writ Appeal has been filed under Section 2(1) of the Madhya Pradesh Uchcha Nyayalay (Khand Nyaypeeth ko Appeal) Adhiniyam, 2005, assailing the order dated 26.11.2024 passed by the learned Single Judge in W.P. No.35549/2024, whereby the Writ Petition has been dismissed. 3. The brief facts of the case are that respondents No.1 to 4 had filed an application under Section 52 of the MPLR Code before the Upper Collector, which was allowed and an interim order was passed in spite of the fact that caveat was filed by the present appellants. Feeling aggrieved the appellants filed W.P. No.35549/2024, which came to be decided finally by order dated 26.11.2024, wherein the appellants had raised grievance that they had filed a Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 2 WA-3005-2024 caveat before the Court of Additional Collector, Chhatarpur yet without giving opportunity of hearing, the interim order passed on 25.10.2024. 4. The learned Single Judge after hearing counsel for the appellants came to the conclusion that the appellants had failed to comply with the requirements of sub-Section (2) of Section 148A of CPC, 1908, which makes it obligatory upon the caveator to serve the notice of caveat by registered post acknowledgment due on the person by whom the application has been filed. 5. Learned counsel for the appellants contended that the order impugned has been passed in contravention to Section 148A of CPC as well as in gross violation of the principles of natural justice. The learned Single Judge also failed to consider that the impugned order dated 25.10.2024 has been passed without serving notice on the caveator despite filing of the caveat. Moreover, the provisions of Section 148A (2) of the CPC are directory in nature and even if the same are not complied, it cannot be a ground to refuse or reject the caveat application and pass interim order without hearing the appellants. Learned counsel for the appellants relied upon a Single Bench decision in the case of State of Karnataka Vs. NIL reported in ILR 1999 KAR 2986 to contend that the right to lodge caveat cannot be taken away on the ground that provisions of sub-Section (2) of Section 148A of CPC are not complied with which are directory in nature. Therefore, the order passed by the learned Single Judge deserves to be set aside. 6. Per contra, learned counsel for the State vehemently opposed the prayer and contended that it is mandatory for the appellants to serve a notice Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 3 WA-3005-2024 of caveat to the opposite party, by whom an application for interim order has been or is going to be moved and unless that condition precedent is satisfied and as per sub-Section (2) of Section 148A Caveator/appellant is duty bound to serve the notice of the lodged caveat to the respondents by whom an application for interim order has been moved against the caveator. He further submitted that since sub-Section (2) of Section 148A of CPC has not been complied, there is no question of compliance of sub-Section (3) of Section 148A of CPC. The learned Single Judge has rightly come to the aforesaid conclusion. In view of the aforesaid, the order passed by the learned Single Judge does not require any interference and accordingly, this appeal is liable to be dismissed. 7. Heard the learned counsel for the parties and perused the record. 8. For the purpose of convenience, Section 148A of CPC is reproduced below: "[148 A. Right to lodge a caveat. -- (1)Where an application is expected to be made, or has been made, in a suit or proceeding instituted, or about to be instituted, in a Court , any person claiming a right to appear before the Court on the hearing of such application may lodge a caveat in respect thereof. (2) Where a caveat has been lodged under sub-section (1), the person by whom the caveat has been lodged (hereinafter referred to as the caveator) shall serve a notice of the caveat by registered post, acknowledgement due, on the person by whom the application has been, or is expected to be, made, under sub-section (1). (3) Where, after a caveat has been lodged under sub- section (1), any application is filed in any suit or proceeding, the Court, shall serve a notice of the application on the caveator. (4) Where a notice of any caveat has been served on the applicant, he shall forthwith furnish the caveator at the caveators expense, with a copy of the application made by him and also with copies of any paper or document Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 4 WA-3005-2024 which has been, or may be, filed by him in support of the application. (5) Where a caveat has been lodged under sub-section (1), such caveat shall not remain in force after the expiry of ninety days from the date on which it was lodged unless the application referred to in sub-section (1) has been made before the expiry of the said period.] 9. Admittedly, the appellants have not complied with the provision of sub-section (2) of Section 148A of CPC by serving notice of caveat by registered post acknowledgement due on the opposite party. Therefore, the learned Single Judge has rightly come to the conclusion that compliance of sub-section (2) of Section 148A of CPC is mandatory in nature and the same has to be followed sequentially, which has not been done in the present case. 10. The object behind the introduction/insertion of the provision of caveat is to safeguard the interest of a person against an order that may be passed on an application filed or expected to be filed by a party in a suit or proceeding instituted or about to be instituted. Such a person lodging a caveat may not be a necessary party to such an application, but he may be affected by an order that may be passed on such an application. Further, another object behind the provision of Section 148A of CPC is to avoid multiplicity of proceedings. For instance, a person, against whom an order is passed on an application in a suit or proceeding to which he is not a party, has to take resort to legal proceedings for the purpose of getting rid of the order. The application referred to in sub-section (1) of Section 148A of CPC may be a substantive application and the person who may be affected by an order that may be passed on such an application may file the caveat for the purpose of Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 5 WA-3005-2024 opposing the application. 11. In the absence of such a form, it may be in the shape of a petition in which the caveator has to specify the nature of the application which is expected to be made or has been made and also his right to appear before the Court on the hearing of such application, under sub-Section (2) of Section 148A , the caveator also has to serve a notice of the caveat on the person by whom the application has been, or is expected to be, made under sub-section (1). Therefore, in the caveat the name of the person who is expected to file or has filed the application, the nature of the application and the right under which the person lodging the caveat claims to appear before the Court have to be stated explicitly. 12. In the present case, the appellants did not serve the copy of the caveat application on the opposite party. Sub section (2) of Section 148A of CPC makes it obligatory upon the caveator to serve a notice of caveat by registered post acknowledgment due on the person by whom the application has been or is expected to be made. Thus, in absence of fulfilment of requirement of sub-section (2) of Section 148A of CPC, the learned Single Judge has rightly come to a conclusion that there is no valid caveat application filed before the Additional Collector necessitating indulgence on the part of Additional Collector. Unless and until requirement of Section 148A (2) of CPC are fulfilled and receipt of sending the application to the proposed the petitioners/appellants is enclosed along with the caveat application, it cannot be said that the same is properly constituted caveat application. Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57 NEUTRAL CITATION NO. 2025:MPHC-JBP:1679 6 WA-3005-2024 13. In the present case, the appellants themselves have failed to make mandatory compliance compliance of sub-Section(2) of Section 148A of CPC, therefore, the interim order passed by the concerned Court cannot be faulted with. The learned Single Judge has rightly interpreted the provisions of Section 148A of CPC and had rightly arrived at a conclusion that sub- section (3) is to be read in sequential manner and until and unless there is compliance of sub-section (2), sub-Section (3) of Section 148A of CPC will not come into operation. 14. Accordingly, this Court is of considered opinion that the order passed by the learned Single Judge is in accordance with law and does not warrant any interference. 15. This appeal being bereft of merits and substance is hereby dismissed. (SUSHRUT ARVIND DHARMADHIKARI) (AVANINDRA KUMAR SINGH) JUDGE JUDGE HS Signature Not Verified Signed by: HEMANT SARAF Signing time: 17-01-2025 14:42:57
